kept.text:
Teradyne昨晚财报，营收12.8亿美元同比翻了接近一倍，EPS 2.56远超市场预期的2.04，创历史新高。盘后跌了12%。

全球半导体测试设备这门生意，本质上只有两个玩家。Teradyne和日本的Advantest合计拿走全球ATE市场约80%的份额，格局维持了很多年，芯片厂在研发阶段选定测试平台后从验证到量产整条链绑死了，切换成本极高。
这次财报数字本身毫无问题。Semi Test首次突破11亿美元单季，同比翻倍。Compute占SoC收入的75%，两年前不到10%，公司已经从mobile测试公司彻底变成AI测试公司。机器人业务连续四个季度环比增长，Non-GAAP营业利润率37.5%，历史最高。

跌的原因在guidance里。

Q2营收指引11.5到12.5亿，中位数比Q1低了将近一成。毛利率从60.9%回落到58%-59%，CFO直接说了，Q1有一次性非经常性收益和峰值出货的杠杆效应，不可持续。全年节奏前高后低，上半年占全年55%-60%，这个区间上沿是三个月前没有的，刚加上去。听起来像好消息，但市场读到的是下半年节奏不确定。CEO自己用了"lumpy growth"这个词。

还有一个数字很说明问题。管理层说Q1拿到了第一笔merchant GPU的多系统量产测试订单，Q2交付。这是突破Advantest在GPU测试垄断地位的起点，意义不小。但同一份prepared remarks里也说了，全年merchant GPU可见收入大约5000万美元。单季营收12.8亿的公司，GPU测试全年5000万，份额渗透才刚起步。Advantest的V93000在NVIDIA和AMD端绑了这么多年，不是一两个季度能松动的。

两家现在走的路线完全不同。Teradyne这季度发了Photon 100做硅光子和共封装光学测试，又发了Omnyx做服务器板级测试，收了TestInsight，跟MultiLane搞了高速I/O合资。所有动作都是沿着"wafer to data center"这条线往纵深走。Advantest反过来，走横向生态联盟路线，拉着FormFactor和Technoprobe在探针卡和handler端做开放平台。哪条路更对，要等硅光子和chiplet测试真正上量才知道。Teradyne自己给的硅光子TAM估计是每年3到7亿美元。

市场去年给了Teradyne一个AI测试高增长的估值锚，股价2025年涨了242%。Q1证明增长是真的，但Q2 guidance告诉市场增速在放缓。70%收入绑定AI，客户集中到两个specifying customer加一个purchasing customer超过10%，这种浓度在关税和贸易政策飘忽不定的时候会被放大。业绩超了但预期差在收敛，beat and drop的经典剧本。

国内长川科技和华峰测控是A股最直接的对标。国产ATE拿了国内市场约25%份额，靠成熟制程平台加三成价格折扣往前推。高端这块跟光刻机的处境差不多，3nm以下、HBM4、AI加速器这些场景的差距短期补不上。更现实的机会在国内先进存储和逻辑扩产周期里测试设备国产化率从低位往上爬，这条路比追绝对技术靠谱。

kept.text:
JPMorgan Upgrades $TER to Overweight from Neutral, PT $400

Analyst comments: "We are upgrading shares of Teradyne to Overweight following the ~20% post-earnings pullback in the stock, versus the S&P flat, with the lumpiness in customer purchasing of test equipment driving limited changes to the long-term drivers and secular catalysts, including the ramp in merchant GPU tests, VIP ASIC test market opportunities, and leverage to the expansion of the memory test TAM, alongside cyclical recovery in Autos & Industrial TAM.

We were on the sidelines on TER shares starting the year and heading into the print, with the premium valuation leaving limited room for upsides to elevated buy-side expectations, which were embedding sequential rises in demand through the year rather than appreciating the lumpiness in customer buying. However, the pullback offers materially higher upside for the shares on a largely unchanged revenue trajectory in our expectations, with moderation in revenues in 2H only reflective of the timing of programs and purchases rather than changing the long-term growth trajectory.

We see a continued robust long-term trajectory with the help of: 1) strengthening custom/VIP ASIC demand, including the aspirations of additional hyperscalers and Neoclouds to develop their own ASICs; 2) an increase in memory TAM, driven by an increase in HBM TAM in conjunction with increasing AI infrastructure demand; 3) incremental testing TAM associated with CPO and SiPh networking equipment; 4) a ramp in share with merchant GPU testers beyond the initial modest contribution in 2026; and 5) recovery in the test market associated with Autos and Industrial markets.

While we acknowledge that the company might not get to its $6B revenue target despite the ATE test TAM reaching $12B in 2027, due to a materially lower share than the implied 50% in the merchant GPU test market, we believe the upside to the shares will stem from reaching the $6B revenue target in relatively short order beyond 2027, with lower than 25% share in the merchant GPU test market on account of upsides in other parts of ATE TAM.

As we look to our revenue forecasts for Teradyne, we see limited changes to our forecasts for FY27, which embedded conservatism around sequential momentum from the high levels of demand in 4Q25 and 1Q26, but we raise our FY28 revenue forecasts on the opportunity stemming from revenue contribution in relation to merchant GPU and CPO/SiPh testing. Our updated earnings forecasts imply earnings growth of 40% in both FY27 and FY28, following 80% growth in FY26, which in our view also justifies a valuation multiple closer to 35x-40x, which would more appropriately incorporate the strong earnings growth outlook alongside the lumpiness and lack of predictability of the business over the short term."

Analyst: Samik Chatterjee

kept.text:
$TER 
Teradyne’s latest earnings were clearly strong.

Both revenue and earnings came in above market expectations, and AI related test demand remained strong. Yet the stock still sold off sharply.

So what exactly did the market see?

Was it simply disappointment around short term guidance, or was the market starting to price in a more structural limitation facing the semiconductor test industry as AI infrastructure becomes more complex?

In this article, I take a closer look at whether this selloff was just a market misunderstanding, or whether it may actually be a signal of opportunity.

Full article: https://t.co/PANEaYEZQY

売上・EPS共に大幅ビート、過去最高を更新。AI関連需要が売上の約70%を占め、半導体テスト需要が爆発的に拡大📈

▶ 時間外 -8%📉

▶ Q1
⭕️ EPS $2.56（+241.3% YoY）🆚予想 $2.11
⭕️ 売上高 $1.282B（+87.0% YoY）🆚予想 $1.21B

▶ Q2ガイダンス
⭕️ EPS $2.01（Mid）🆚予想 $1.94
⭕️ 売上高 $1.20B（Mid）🆚予想 $1.17B

▶ セグメント別売上高
・ Semiconductor Test $1.111B
・ Robotics $91M
・ Product Test $80M

🔑 KPI（重要指標）
・ GAAP粗利率：60.9%（前年同期 60.6%）
・ Non-GAAP営業利益率：37.5%（前年同期 20.5%）
・ Non-GAAP営業利益：$480.4M（前年同期 $140.8M、+241%）
・ DIO：66日（5年平均比▲23日、在庫過剰の懸念なし）
・ 営業キャッシュフロー：$265M（前年同期比 +64%）
・ AI関連売上比率：約70%（Q4の60%超→さらに加速）

📍 決算の注目ポイント
・ 売上・EPS・営業利益のすべてが四半期過去最高を更新。AI関連テスト需要の構造的拡大を改めて確認
・ カスタムASIC（超大手向け）テスト市場でシェア約50%を維持。UltraFLEXplusが高性能AIチップテストの主力プラットフォームとして定着
・ 「ウェーハからAIデータセンターまで」の一貫戦略が功を奏し、コンピュート・ネットワーキング・メモリ全ての需要が同時上昇
・ ロボティクスは前四半期まで構造改革（リストラ）を実施、Q1では黒字安定化へ。Universal Robots × Scale AIのAIトレーナー連携が新たな成長軸に
・ Q2ガイダンス中央値$1.20B（前年同期比+84%）は引き続き力強く、アップサイクルの継続を示唆

▶ CEO コメント（Greg Smith）
「Q1業績は新記録を達成した。売上の約70%がAI関連需要によるもので、ウェーハからAIデータセンターに至る我々の戦略の強さを示している。半導体テスト・製品テスト・ロボティクス全セグメントで力強い前年比成長を実現し、AIが牽引する堅調な勢いをもって今後も継続する見通しだ」

kept.text:
$TER KEY READ-THROUGHS FROM TERADYNE Q1 2026 EARNINGS CALL

Teradyne’s Q1 2026 earnings call is a high-signal cross-market data point for AI infrastructure because the company sits close to actual device production ramps rather than upstream capex intentions. The material confirms that AI-related semiconductor demand has broadened from general-purpose accelerators into memory, networking, silicon photonics, power management, board test, HDD, robotics, and data-center operations. The positive read-through is that AI infrastructure demand remains structurally strong and is generating incremental test intensity across a widening set of device categories. The negative read-through is that revenue timing is increasingly concentrated in a smaller number of very large vertically integrated customers and device programs, creating meaningful H2 linearity risk for AI hardware names whose valuations assume uninterrupted sequential growth. The highest-conviction portfolio implication is a barbell: strong longer-duration fundamental support for AI infrastructure suppliers, but elevated near-term trading risk where expectations embed smooth H2 shipment growth.

SEMICONDUCTOR CAPITAL EQUIPMENT AND AI HARDWARE CADENCE

AI HARDWARE DEMAND IS REAL, BUT H2 LINEARITY EXPECTATIONS LOOK TOO AGGRESSIVE (READ-THROUGH 1)

Affected companies: NVIDIA Corp (NVDA: USA), Advanced Micro Devices Inc (AMD: USA), Broadcom Inc (AVGO: USA), Marvell Technology Inc (MRVL: USA), Arista Networks Inc (ANET: USA), Vertiv Holdings Co (VRT: USA), Super Micro Computer Inc (SMCI: USA), Dell Technologies Inc (DELL: USA), Micron Technology Inc (MU: USA), Coherent Corp (COHR: USA).

Directional impact and magnitude: Long-duration positive, high. Near-term trading impact mixed to negative, medium, for high-beta AI infrastructure names priced for linear Q/Q growth.

Call support: Teradyne stated that AI-related demand was “nearly 70% of our revenue, up from about 60% in Q4 of 2025,” and guided Q2 revenue to $1.15B to $1.25B, representing roughly 84% year-over-year growth at the midpoint. However, management also guided that “approximately 55% to 60% of annual revenue” is expected in H1 and stated that Q4 remains partly undefined. Greg Smith also said VIP compute is “most first-half weighted,” with the next wave of next-generation technology expected in early 2027, possibly bleeding into late 2026.

Transmission mechanism: Teradyne’s testers are ordered close to actual wafer and device ramps, so its H1/H2 commentary is a closer-to-production signal than front-end WFE capex commentary. The call confirms strong AI silicon demand, but also indicates that the current VIP compute surge may be followed by a timing gap before the next program wave. This creates risk for AI hardware supply-chain names if investors extrapolate Q1/Q2 strength into uninterrupted H2 acceleration. The risk is not secular demand destruction; it is revenue phasing, production acceptance, and customer-specific program cadence.

Near-term trading catalyst: Positive headline read-through from the record Q1 print and Q2 guide, but negative risk to stocks whose estimates require continued H2 sequential growth.

Longer-duration fundamental shift: AI demand is broadening and stacking across general-purpose AI data centers, inference-optimized silicon, and eventually edge/physical AI, supporting a durable multi-year AI infrastructure cycle.

ATE TAM EXPANSION AND GPU DUAL-SOURCING FAVOR TERADYNE, WHILE ADVANTEST FACES SHARE-RISK DESPITE POSITIVE TAM (READ-THROUGH 2)

Affected companies: Teradyne Inc (TER: USA), Advantest Corp (6857: Japan).

Directional impact and magnitude: Teradyne positive, high. Advantest mixed, medium to high: positive from ATE TAM expansion, negative from potential share dilution in merchant GPU, custom ASIC, and CPO.

Call support: Teradyne reported record Q1 revenue of approximately $1.3B, Semi Test revenue above $1B for the 1st time, and compute at roughly 75% of SoC product revenue. Management also stated: “We have received our first multi-system production test orders for merchant GPU in Q1,” with systems expected to ship, install, and enter production in Q2. On GPU share, Greg Smith said Teradyne is moving from low-single-digit share in 2026 toward a possible “30% to 70% range over the mid-term” in dual-source customer environments.

Transmission mechanism: AI accelerator complexity is increasing test intensity, expanding the ATE TAM. Teradyne’s production GPU order is strategically important because it means the platform qualification and library conversion hurdle has been crossed. The next phase is “fast follower” SKU conversion, which could gradually reduce incumbent test-platform share. Advantest still benefits from a larger TAM and likely incumbent position in high-end AI compute, but the call increases confidence that large AI customers want test-equipment supply-chain resilience and multi-sourcing.

Near-term trading catalyst: Q2 merchant GPU shipment and production acceptance are important proof points for Teradyne’s GPU share-gain thesis.

Longer-duration fundamental shift: Large AI customers appear increasingly willing to dual-source test platforms, reducing incumbent lock-in and making throughput, reliability, test coverage, and responsiveness more important than installed-base incumbency.

MERCHANT GPU TEST ORDERS CONFIRM REAL PRODUCTION CAPACITY EXPANSION, BUT THE 2026 REVENUE CONTRIBUTION IS STILL MODEST (READ-THROUGH 3)

Affected companies: NVIDIA Corp (NVDA: USA), Advanced Micro Devices Inc (AMD: USA), Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co (2330: Taiwan), ASE Technology Holding Co (3711: Taiwan), Amkor Technology Inc (AMKR: USA).

Directional impact and magnitude: Positive, medium near term; positive, high longer term.

Call support: Teradyne received its 1st “multi-system production test orders for merchant GPU” in Q1 and expects the systems to “ship, be installed and be in-production in Q2.” Management also said it has line of sight to approximately $50M of merchant GPU revenue for 2026, but H2 visibility remains limited.

Transmission mechanism: Merchant GPU tester orders are a downstream production-readiness indicator. Test capacity is needed only when device volumes are moving toward production; therefore, Teradyne’s order commentary supports continued GPU capacity expansion at the foundry, OSAT, and system-integration layers. However, $50M of line-of-sight revenue is still small relative to Teradyne’s total expected 2026 revenue, so the call should not be interpreted as evidence of a massive immediate incremental GPU ramp. The more important implication is that the production qualification process is underway, which lowers bottleneck risk for future GPU volumes and supports broader supply-chain throughput.

Near-term trading catalyst: Positive sentiment for the GPU supply chain from Q2 production deployment.

Longer-duration fundamental shift: AI accelerator customers may increasingly treat test capacity and test-platform diversity as strategic supply-chain variables, not merely back-end manufacturing details.

CUSTOM ASIC AND INFERENCE SILICON BENEFICIARIES GAIN CONVICTION, WHILE GENERAL-PURPOSE GPU SHARE OF AI COMPUTE FACES SLOW MIX PRESSURE (READ-THROUGH 4)

Affected companies: Broadcom Inc (AVGO: USA), Marvell Technology Inc (MRVL: USA), Alphabet Inc (GOOGL: USA), https://t.co/SpqvHNUxpK Inc (AMZN: USA), Microsoft Corp (MSFT: USA), Meta Platforms Inc (META: USA), Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co (2330: Taiwan), NVIDIA Corp (NVDA: USA).

Directional impact and magnitude: Broadcom and Marvell positive, high. Hyperscalers positive, medium. TSMC positive, medium. NVIDIA long-duration mixed to negative, medium, from inference mix diversification rather than near-term demand weakness.

Call support: Management described AI as 3 waves: general-purpose AI data-center capacity, inference-optimized compute silicon, and later edge/physical AI. Greg Smith said that in 2026 “we are entering the second wave,” where AI data centers are augmented with “compute silicon optimized for inference at-scale.” In Q&A, he said there are currently 2 hyperscaler compute ASIC programs at scale, and Teradyne is competing for additional unramped parts and dual-source status, with timing “more 2027 than 2026.” He also cited active opportunities in Arm-based data-center CPUs and devices optimized for inference and agentic workloads.

Transmission mechanism: Custom ASICs and inference-optimized silicon create direct demand for design services, networking silicon, advanced packaging, foundry capacity, and test capacity. Broadcom and Marvell are the clearest public-market beneficiaries because they are leveraged to custom silicon, networking, and high-speed interconnect. Hyperscalers benefit through potential inference cost-per-token improvements and better workload-specific silicon economics. NVIDIA remains structurally strong because the 1st AI wave is still large, but the call supports the thesis that more inference workloads will migrate toward optimized silicon over time, creating mix pressure on general-purpose GPU dependency.

Near-term trading catalyst: Limited, because management pointed to 2027 timing for many custom ASIC share opportunities.

Longer-duration fundamental shift: AI compute architecture is moving from a GPU-dominant training buildout toward a heterogeneous mix of GPUs, custom ASICs, Arm CPUs, high-speed networking, and inference-specific devices.

MEMORY, STORAGE, AND MOBILE

HBM, DRAM, AND SSD TEST STRENGTH IS A HIGH-CONVICTION POSITIVE FOR MEMORY SUPPLIERS (READ-THROUGH 5)

Affected companies: Micron Technology Inc (MU: USA), SK Hynix Inc (000660: Korea), Samsung Electronics Co (005930: Korea), Western Digital Corp (WDC: USA).

Directional impact and magnitude: Positive, high for HBM/DRAM-exposed suppliers; positive, medium for NAND/SSD-exposed suppliers.

Call support: Teradyne reported Q1 memory revenue of $203M, roughly flat with the record Q4 level, and stated that memory test demand is “even stronger than our view in January,” driven by AI compute demand for HBM and DRAM. Management also said it is beginning to see “increasing flash test demand-driven by SSD,” and that memory may be more back-half weighted than front-half weighted.

Transmission mechanism: Memory test demand is closely tied to capacity additions, product transitions, and test-time expansion. HBM and high-performance DRAM require more complex and higher-value testing than commodity memory, while SSD-related flash test demand indicates AI infrastructure storage tiers are also driving NAND activity. The H2-weighted memory commentary is especially important because it may offset VIP compute digestion later in the year. This is a positive read-through for memory revenue durability, mix, pricing discipline, and capex intensity.

Near-term trading catalyst: Positive for memory names if H2 memory test orders continue to fill in.

Longer-duration fundamental shift: AI compute is increasing memory test intensity, not merely memory bit demand, which supports a higher structural equipment and manufacturing complexity baseline for HBM and advanced DRAM.

AI-DRIVEN HDD EXABYTE GROWTH IS A POSITIVE READ-THROUGH FOR NEARLINE STORAGE (READ-THROUGH 6)

Affected companies: Seagate Technology Holdings (STX: USA), Western Digital Corp (WDC: USA), Micron Technology Inc (MU: USA), Samsung Electronics Co (005930: Korea), SK Hynix Inc (000660: Korea).

Directional impact and magnitude: Seagate and Western Digital positive, high. NAND suppliers positive, medium.

Call support: Management stated that the IST business is seeing strength in HDD driven by “greater than 20% annual exabyte growth fueled by AI,” which translates into “longer test times per drive and a larger HDD TAM.” Teradyne also cited increasing flash test demand driven by SSD.

Transmission mechanism: AI data-center workloads generate large storage requirements across training data, model checkpoints, inference logs, retrieval systems, backups, and cold/warm storage. Nearline HDD demand benefits from exabyte growth, while longer test times indicate higher drive capacity and complexity. SSD demand also benefits as higher-performance storage tiers scale alongside AI servers. Teradyne’s test-demand commentary provides a production-side confirmation that AI storage demand is translating into equipment and manufacturing activity, not only headline cloud capex.

Near-term trading catalyst: Positive for storage suppliers if H2 IST growth materializes.

Longer-duration fundamental shift: AI is reinforcing a 2-tier storage architecture: high-capacity HDD for exabyte-scale storage and SSD/NAND for performance-sensitive AI data pipelines.

MOBILE WEAKNESS OUTSIDE IOS IS A NEGATIVE READ-THROUGH FOR ANDROID SEMIS AND HANDSET OEMS (READ-THROUGH 7)

Affected companies: Qualcomm Inc (QCOM: USA), MediaTek Inc (2454: Taiwan), Samsung Electronics Co (005930: Korea), Xiaomi Corp (1810: Hong Kong), Qorvo Inc (QRVO: USA), Skyworks Solutions Inc (SWKS: USA), Apple Inc (AAPL: USA).

Directional impact and magnitude: Negative, medium for Android-exposed semis and OEMs. Relative positive, low to medium for Apple because management specifically called out weakness outside the iOS ecosystem.

Call support: Teradyne stated: “Mobile appears a bit weaker with memory pricing and availability affecting end-market demand, especially outside the iOS ecosystem.” Management also said mobile revenue was roughly flat with Q4 2025 and remains muted in the overall SoC portfolio.

Transmission mechanism: Higher memory pricing and tighter availability raise smartphone bill-of-materials pressure, particularly for price-sensitive Android OEMs. That can delay model launches, constrain channel restocking, pressure margins, or reduce unit elasticity. Teradyne’s flat mobile test commentary indicates no meaningful mobile-driven test equipment recovery. The call therefore supports a cautious stance on Android handset semis and RF suppliers, while implying Apple’s procurement scale and ecosystem positioning may be relatively less exposed.

Near-term trading catalyst: Negative for Android smartphone supply-chain estimates if memory tightness continues.

Longer-duration fundamental shift: Mobile is no longer the primary driver of Teradyne’s test cycle, reducing the relevance of smartphone recovery as a broader semiconductor capex offset.

NETWORKING, OPTICAL, AND INTERCONNECT

SILICON PHOTONICS AND CPO HAVE MOVED FROM LAB TO FAB, SUPPORTING OPTICAL NETWORKING AND PHOTONICS BENEFICIARIES (READ-THROUGH 8)

Affected companies: Broadcom Inc (AVGO: USA), Marvell Technology Inc (MRVL: USA), Coherent Corp (COHR: USA), Lumentum Holdings Inc (LITE: USA), Fabrinet (FN: USA), Credo Technology Group Holding Ltd (CRDO: USA), Arista Networks Inc (ANET: USA), Teradyne Inc (TER: USA), Advantest Corp (6857: Japan).

Directional impact and magnitude: Positive, medium near term; positive, high longer term.

Call support: Teradyne introduced Photon 100 for silicon photonics and co-packaged optics testing and described it as bringing testing “from lab to fab.” Management estimated the current 2026 silicon photonics market around $100M and the midterm equipment opportunity at $300M to $700M annually. Greg Smith said scale-out networking is likely the primary 2027 driver, while higher-volume scale-up networking is more likely in 2028 and 2029.

Transmission mechanism: AI clusters are increasingly constrained by bandwidth, latency, signal integrity, and power. Silicon photonics and CPO directly address these constraints by moving optical connectivity closer to compute and switching silicon. This benefits switch ASIC vendors, custom silicon suppliers, optical component vendors, photonics packaging suppliers, and high-speed networking platforms. Teradyne’s Photon 100 launch and CPO commentary indicate that optical interconnect is entering production-oriented test workflows, which is a stronger signal than a purely R&D-stage announcement.

Near-term trading catalyst: Positive for optical and networking names as investors gain evidence that CPO and silicon photonics are moving into production infrastructure.

Longer-duration fundamental shift: Scale-out and later scale-up AI networking should create a multi-year shift toward higher optical content per cluster, with test, packaging, alignment, and yield becoming critical bottlenecks.

Geographic and supplier signal: Management described a 4-way ecosystem involving a foundry, end customer, ficonTEC, and Teradyne, with teams in Taiwan, Israel, Germany, and North America. This confirms that CPO commercialization remains a globally distributed supply-chain problem rather than a single-vendor product cycle.

BACKPLANE AND MID-PLANE INTERCONNECT ARCHITECTURES CREATE MIX RISK FOR CABLE-CENTRIC AI CONNECTIVITY EXPOSURE (READ-THROUGH 9)

Affected companies: Amphenol Corp (APH: USA), TE Connectivity Ltd (TEL: USA), Credo Technology Group Holding Ltd (CRDO: USA), Broadcom Inc (AVGO: USA), Marvell Technology Inc (MRVL: USA), Arista Networks Inc (ANET: USA).

Directional impact and magnitude: Positive, medium for high-speed connector, backplane, mid-plane, and signal-integrity beneficiaries. Mixed, medium for companies with meaningful active-cable exposure if investor expectations over-index on cable-based cluster architectures.

Call support: Teradyne stated that its multi-lane test products joint venture should accelerate high-speed I/O and data-center interconnect test solutions as AI data centers transition “from cable-based connections to backplane and mid-plane architectures.”

Transmission mechanism: AI cluster networking architecture is evolving toward denser, more integrated, and higher-speed interconnect. That supports companies exposed to high-speed connectors, backplanes, signal integrity, retimers, switch ASICs, and optical/electrical validation. It also creates mix risk for suppliers whose investment theses depend heavily on cable-based architectures remaining the dominant interconnect layer. The impact is not necessarily negative for all active-cable suppliers, but it does argue that the market should distinguish between durable high-speed connectivity exposure and narrower cable-product exposure.

Near-term trading catalyst: Limited, because architecture transitions occur over product generations.

Longer-duration fundamental shift: AI data-center interconnect is moving from modular cable-heavy designs toward more integrated backplane/mid-plane architectures, increasing the strategic importance of signal integrity, test, and system-level validation.

ANALOG, POWER, AND INDUSTRIAL SEMIS

DATA-CENTER POWER MANAGEMENT IS OUTGROWING THE BROADER AUTO/INDUSTRIAL CYCLE (READ-THROUGH 10)

Affected companies: Monolithic Power Systems Inc (MPWR: USA), Texas Instruments Inc (TXN: USA), Analog Devices Inc (ADI: USA), Infineon Technologies AG (IFX: Germany), ON Semiconductor Corp (ON: USA), STMicroelectronics NV (STM: USA), NXP Semiconductors NV (NXPI: USA), Vertiv Holdings Co (VRT: USA).

Directional impact and magnitude: Positive, high for data-center power-exposed suppliers such as Monolithic Power. Mixed, medium for broader analog and auto/industrial semis. Positive, medium for data-center power and thermal infrastructure exposed names such as Vertiv.

Call support: Teradyne said auto and industrial revenue nearly doubled sequentially from a low base, driven by “power management demand increases for AI data center build-outs.” Management also stated that 46% of auto and industrial revenue came from data-center devices in Q1, and Greg Smith said “data center is hot, hot, hot in that segment.” Conversely, he said that outside data center, customers are seeing lower inventories and potential demand increases, but Teradyne has not yet seen that translate into increased capital equipment demand.

Transmission mechanism: AI servers and accelerators require significantly more power management, conversion, monitoring, and thermal-control content. Teradyne’s auto/industrial segment strength is therefore a positive signal for data-center power silicon, but not for a broad-based analog recovery. The call indicates the industrial/auto recovery is still narrow and AI-specific, which matters for diversified analog names where investors may be expecting a broader cyclical inflection.

Near-term trading catalyst: Positive for power-management names with AI server exposure; negative or mixed for broad analog names if expectations assume general industrial recovery.

Longer-duration fundamental shift: AI data-center power density is becoming a structural semiconductor content driver, while traditional auto/industrial demand remains more cyclical and less visibly recovered.

SERVER HARDWARE, ODM, AND BACK-END TEST

AI SERVER BOARD AND TRAY COMPLEXITY IS CREATING YIELD RISK FOR OEM/ODM, POSITIVE FOR TEST AUTOMATION (READ-THROUGH 11)

Affected companies: Super Micro Computer Inc (SMCI: USA), Dell Technologies Inc (DELL: USA), Quanta Computer Inc (2382: Taiwan), Wiwynn Corp (6669: Taiwan), Hon Hai Precision Industry Co (2317: Taiwan), Celestica Inc (CLS: Canada), Jabil Inc (JBL: USA), Flex Ltd (FLEX: USA), Teradyne Inc (TER: USA), Keysight Technologies Inc (KEYS: USA).

Directional impact and magnitude: Negative, medium near term for AI server OEM/ODM names exposed to build complexity, rework, and acceptance delays. Positive, medium for board-test, validation, and automation suppliers.

Call support: Teradyne introduced Omnyx, a production board test platform for “serverboards and tray assemblies,” using power, thermal, optical, and TDR capabilities to enable earlier detection of defects “plaguing the build-out of AI data centers.” Michelle Turner also cited possible “hiccups in the AI data center build-out ecosystem” that could affect tester acceptance timing.

Transmission mechanism: AI server architectures are becoming more complex at the board, tray, thermal, optical, and interconnect layers. Complexity raises yield-loss, rework, and schedule-risk for OEMs and ODMs. Earlier and more integrated test can mitigate those risks, but the need for Omnyx is itself evidence that quality and defect issues are real. This is a negative near-term margin and delivery-risk signal for AI server builders, while positive for suppliers that sell validation, test automation, and production-quality tools.

Near-term trading catalyst: Negative for AI server stocks if customer acceptance, yield, or rework issues appear in reported margins or deferred revenue.

Longer-duration fundamental shift: AI server manufacturing is becoming a test-intensive production process, increasing the value of differentiated quality control and system-level validation.

BACK-END TEST CELL BOTTLENECKS ARE A POSITIVE DEMAND SIGNAL FOR PROBE, HANDLER, AND OSAT SUPPLIERS, BUT TIMING VOLATILITY IS HIGH (READ-THROUGH 12)

Affected companies: FormFactor Inc (FORM: USA), Cohu Inc (COHU: USA), ASE Technology Holding Co (3711: Taiwan), Amkor Technology Inc (AMKR: USA), Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co (2330: Taiwan), Teradyne Inc (TER: USA), Advantest Corp (6857: Japan).

Directional impact and magnitude: Positive, medium for probe card, handler, OSAT, and advanced test ecosystem suppliers. Near-term timing risk negative, medium.

Call support: Teradyne said it has more than doubled UltraFLEXplus shipments over the last 9 months while sustaining 12- to 16-week lead times. Michelle Turner also noted that revenue timing can be affected if “all the parts of a test cell are not coming together.” Greg Smith said customers are increasingly focused on “supply-chain resilience all the way back to their test equipment capital supplier.”

Transmission mechanism: Tester deployment requires a full ecosystem: probe cards, handlers, sockets, thermal systems, OSAT capacity, validation software, and customer acceptance. Strong tester shipments therefore create positive demand pull for FormFactor, Cohu, ASE, and Amkor. At the same time, if any element of the test cell is unavailable, revenue can shift across weeks, quarters, or even years. The call supports a positive demand view for the back-end ecosystem but also argues for higher volatility in quarterly revenue recognition.

Near-term trading catalyst: Positive for back-end equipment suppliers if AI test-cell deployments continue; negative if bottlenecks delay revenue recognition.

Longer-duration fundamental shift: Back-end test is becoming a strategic capacity constraint in AI semiconductors, not a commoditized downstream process.

quoted_tweet.text:
$TER EXECUTIVE CALL SUMMARY: Teradyne, Inc. (02/03/26)

Q4 2025 represented a material inflection in both financial performance and narrative framing, with results and forward outlook dominated by AI-driven compute and memory test demand. Reported Q4 revenue was $1,083 million and non-GAAP EPS was $1.80, delivering 41% sequential growth and 44% year-on-year growth in revenue, with non-GAAP EPS up 112% sequentially and 89% year-on-year. Results exceeded the prior-quarter guidance range of $920 million to $1,000 million revenue and $1.20 to $1.46 non-GAAP EPS, implying an 8% revenue beat versus the high end and a 23% EPS beat versus the high end, with the magnitude of the beat consistent with management’s repeated emphasis that AI-related demand trends “continued through the end-of-the year.” (Teradyne, Inc.)

The call’s near-term increment was the Q1 2026 guide, framed as a new quarterly record. Guidance of $1,150 million to $1,250 million revenue and $1.89 to $2.25 non-GAAP EPS implied 11% sequential revenue growth at the midpoint off an already elevated Q4 baseline, and 75% year-on-year growth versus Q1 2025 revenue of $686 million. Gross margin and operating leverage were guided to improve further, with gross margin guided to 58.5%-59.5% and operating profit rate guided to 32% at the midpoint. The forward setup was explicitly positioned as AI-led, with management stating, “Q1 sales are expected to be between $1.15 billion and $1.25 billion, which would be a new quarterly record driven by all things AI,” and with the CEO asserting that “upwards of 70% of our revenue will be driven by AI applications” in Q1. (Teradyne, Inc.)

The strategic increment was the introduction of a re-anchored target earnings model tied to an ATE TAM scenario rather than a fixed year, explicitly reflecting uncertainty around program timing and quarter-to-quarter volatility. Management framed a mid-term ATE TAM of $12 billion to $14 billion (versus about $9 billion in 2025) and outlined an internal model at roughly $6 billion of revenue with non-GAAP EPS of $9.50 to $11, gross margin of 59%-61%, and operating margin of 30%-34%. This reframing raised the level of implied mid-term earnings power while simultaneously emphasizing lumpiness and reduced forecast precision, reinforcing a core tension in the investment debate: structurally higher earnings power under AI-driven test intensity versus heightened volatility and higher dependency on concentrated AI infrastructure spending. 

Q4 2025 PERFORMANCE AND BEAT VS GUIDE

Q4 printed $1,083 million of revenue, comprised of $883 million Semiconductor Test, $110 million Product Test, and $89 million Robotics. Q4 non-GAAP net income was $283 million, or $1.80 per diluted share. The CEO summarized the quarter as: “Teradyne had a strong fourth quarter with 41% sequential revenue growth and more than 100% non-GAAP earnings growth. Both revenue and EPS were above our high guidance…” The scale of the upside relative to the prior-quarter narrative is notable because the Q3 release explicitly expected Q4 sales to increase 25% sequentially and 27% year-on-year; the actual outcome was 41% sequential and 44% year-on-year, implying a substantial acceleration of AI-related ordering strength late in the year and/or a favorable shift in shipment timing and customer pull-ins. (Teradyne, Inc.)

Profitability demonstrated meaningful operating leverage despite a gross margin that was described as pressured by mix and discrete items. On the call, non-GAAP gross margin was reported at 57.2%, with offsets cited from lower Product Test margins, Robotics mix, and an inventory write-down on legacy products. Operating expenses were $306 million on a non-GAAP basis and the operating profit rate was 29% (non-GAAP operating profit dollars of $314 million, described as roughly doubling versus both prior quarter and prior year). The combination of slightly compressed gross margin with sharply higher operating profit underscores that incremental gross profit dollars from Semiconductor Test volume were sufficiently large to overwhelm the fixed-cost structure. This dynamic matters for forward modeling because the Q1 guide implies further margin expansion (58.5%-59.5% gross margin, 32% operating margin midpoint), suggesting that the Q4 gross margin headwinds were not positioned as structural. (Teradyne, Inc.)

Cash generation and capital return were positioned as supportive but potentially cyclical in sustainability. Q4 free cash flow was cited at $219 million, and $204 million was returned via repurchases and dividends. For FY 2025, free cash flow was $450 million, while $785 million was returned (174% of free cash flow), implying an aggressive capital return posture, partially enabled by balance sheet capacity rather than purely current-period cash generation. The call reinforced “dry powder” for M&A while remaining opportunistic on buybacks, but the magnitude of capital return relative to free cash flow indicates an elevated reliance on the durability of the upcycle and/or willingness to run lower cash balances in the near term. (Teradyne, Inc.)

A technical note on the transcript: the draft transcript line referencing “Fourth quarter sales were $1.83” is internally inconsistent with the segment totals and the earnings release; the earnings release reports $1,083 million, which reconciles precisely to the $883 million Semiconductor Test, $110 million Product Test, and $89 million Robotics segment totals. (Teradyne, Inc.)

DEMAND DRIVERS AND MIX SHIFT

The principal narrative of the call was an accelerated pivot to AI-driven test demand across compute, memory, and adjacent data-center related categories. The CEO emphasized a sharp increase in AI exposure: “AI demand drove 40% to 50% of our revenue in Q3. In Q4, AI drove more than 60% of our revenue. Looking forward to Q1 of 2026, we expect that upwards of 70% of our revenue will be driven by AI applications.” This level of AI-derived revenue concentration is materially higher than historical Teradyne end-market distributions and implies that near-term earnings are increasingly levered to the pace and continuity of AI infrastructure build-out, as well as to the test intensity of specific high-performance devices and platforms. The call also broadened the AI linkage beyond SoC and memory test into “power management, SLT, HDD, ICT and optical test,” implying that AI infrastructure is increasing test intensity and/or deployment volume across multiple layers of the hardware stack. In practical terms, this framing expands the set of Teradyne businesses positioned to monetize AI capex, potentially reducing single-product dependency within Semiconductor Test while increasing dependency on the broader AI cycle.

The call also reinforced a structural end-market rebalancing away from the 2020-2021 mobile-heavy era. Management stated that compute is now the largest component of revenue and grew 90% year-on-year in 2025, contrasting with prior years where mobile dominated SoC, memory, and wireless test. The CEO described SoC revenue mix shifting from 2023 (about 10% compute, 50% auto/industrial, 40% mobile) to 2025 (nearly 50% compute, with auto/industrial and mobile roughly balanced at about 25% each). This diversification was explicitly framed as de-risking the new target earnings model, although the simultaneous claim that compute is both larger and highly concentrated suggests that de-risking is occurring primarily via reduced dependence on mobile cyclicality, not via reduced customer concentration risk.

SEGMENT DEEP DIVE

SEMICONDUCTOR TEST (SOCTEST, MEMORY, IST)

Semiconductor Test was the dominant driver of Q4 growth, delivering $883 million of revenue in Q4 (versus $606 million in Q3), implying 46% sequential growth at the segment level based on reported segment totals. Within Semiconductor Test, management provided additional call-level detail: SoC revenue was cited at $647 million (up 47% sequentially) and memory revenue at $206 million (up 61% sequentially), with memory described as a record sales quarter. This mix is consistent with the CEO’s framing that the “striking trend” was the increase in AI-driven revenue in the 2H 2025 timeframe and that AI demand is “obvious in compute and memory.” (Teradyne, Inc.)

Several high-signal points were embedded in management commentary and Q&A. First, the CEO characterized the VIP compute market as highly concentrated with only a few players driving significant ATE purchases, explicitly attributing “revenue lumpiness” and “forecasting” difficulty to this concentration. This is a critical investment implication: the step-up in earnings power is simultaneously accompanied by a higher probability of sharp quarterly swings driven by program timing, procurement cadence, and ramp schedules at a small number of hyperscaler/custom ASIC ecosystems. Second, management asserted that it maintained about 50% share in VIP compute in 2025 but stated “no share is safe,” emphasizing a competitive dual-sourcing dynamic where incumbency is less durable and feature-performance reliability can reallocate share each generation. This framing suggests both upside potential (share expansion off a “much lower share position” in some compute categories) and a non-trivial risk of share erosion even during a secular upcycle if competitors close capability gaps.

Third, the CEO quantified 2025 SoC TAM composition in Q&A as roughly $5 billion compute, about $1 billion mobility, just under $1 billion auto/industrial, and about $700 million service, while caveating that third-party final numbers would be available later. This implies compute represented the vast majority of the SoC test opportunity in 2025 and that incremental TAM volatility will be dominated by compute device programs. Notably, these figures are approximate and imply a higher aggregate than the “7.2” SoC TAM referenced by an analyst question, reinforcing that the precision of TAM sizing is inherently limited in the moment and may vary depending on whether service is included in the SoC TAM figure.

Fourth, memory test commentary suggested a more measured but still positive forward setup. Management stated the overall memory TAM was down about 4% in 2025 versus 2024, with share gains achieved, and stated that DRAM and HBM now comprise nearly 90% of the memory TAM (versus a prior 50/50 split between DRAM and flash in 2020-2021). For 2026, management anticipated low-2-digit memory TAM growth driven by HBM and DRAM and expected incremental share gains. This implies that while compute is the largest swing factor, memory could provide a more persistent growth vector if HBM and AI-related DRAM demand continues to expand and if Teradyne’s “final test” positioning remains advantaged.

Fifth, the Integrated Systems Test business was described as having grown over 50% from 2024 to 2025, with meaningful diversification away from historical single-customer concentration in HDD and mobile SLT. The CEO cited new customer ramps in mobile SLT, entry into compute SLT, and late-2025 HDD orders from a new customer expected to ramp in 2026. In Q&A, management stated HDD revenue is expected to increase about 2x between 2025 and 2026. The strategic relevance is 2-fold: (1) SLT and system-level insertions are positioned as a structural response to higher device complexity and quality requirements; (2) IST revenue is not fully captured in conventional ATE TAM definitions, complicating “share of TAM” comparisons and enabling upside that may not be reflected in ATE-centric industry tracking.

PRODUCT TEST AND PHOTONICS / DATA CENTER ADJACENCIES

Product Test revenue in Q4 was $110 million (versus $88 million in Q3), implying 25% sequential growth. Management described Product Test Group growth in 2025 as 8% year-on-year, driven by defense and aerospace, and positioned 2026 as a growth year across all Product Test business lines, including benefits from the integration of Quantifi Photonics and cross-training with LitePoint. The strategic framing emphasized that cloud and edge AI build-out is driving demand not only for compute devices but also for optical test and other infrastructure test nodes, potentially broadening the monetization surface area of AI capex. (Teradyne, Inc.)

A key strategic announcement adjacent to Product Test was the joint venture with MultiLane. The JV is intended to accelerate test solutions for high-speed data connections in AI data center equipment. Teradyne will be the majority owner and MultiLane will contribute assets related to its test and measurement business; closing is expected in 1H 2026. On the call, management guided to consolidation of the JV into Product Test results, with net income attributable to non-controlling interest disclosed separately, and characterized 2026 EPS impact as de minimis but accretive. The near-term financial contribution is therefore positioned as modest, while the strategic value is framed around expanding coverage “from wafer level to the data center” and deepening the portfolio in high-speed interconnect test, a key bottleneck area as compute fabrics migrate to higher bandwidth and more complex interconnect standards. (Teradyne, Inc.)

ROBOTICS

Robotics revenue in Q4 was $89 million (versus $75 million in Q3), implying 19% sequential growth and marking the 3rd consecutive quarter of growth as described by management. The segment remains comparatively small versus Semiconductor Test, but management highlighted a large e-commerce customer as an early indicator of AI-driven robotics demand, with “greater than 5%” of robotics revenue in Q4 attributed to this customer and expectation that this revenue could increase ~3x from 2025 to 2026, with continued growth beyond 2026 as deployments expand. The profitability narrative was explicit: breakeven is targeted for Robotics in 2026, with positive contribution beyond. This implies that Robotics is being treated as an option value on “physical AI” adoption while near-term margins remain sensitive to mix, execution, and implementation complexity reduction.

Q1 2026 OUTLOOK AND VISIBILITY

Q1 2026 guidance was the dominant valuation driver emerging from the call: revenue $1,150 million to $1,250 million and non-GAAP EPS $1.89 to $2.25, with gross margin expected at 58.5%-59.5% and operating profit rate 32% at the midpoint. At the midpoint, this implies 11% sequential growth versus Q4 and 75% year-on-year growth versus Q1 2025. The guidance did not include merchant GPU revenue, with the CEO stating: “Our guidance for Q1 does not include any merchant GPU revenue,” and characterizing GPU-related revenue as more likely a 2H 2026 factor following production qualification. (Teradyne, Inc.)

Despite the strength of Q1 guidance, management repeatedly emphasized limited visibility into 2H 2026 and explicitly warned against extrapolation. The CFO referenced stronger backlog and better 1H visibility versus the prior year, while the CEO stated that visibility into 2H is not strong and cautioned about the possibility of a surge followed by digestion. This is a key risk signal: the company is signaling that near-term demand may be front-loaded and that the AI infrastructure build-out could include ordering pauses that create volatility even if the secular trend remains favorable.

Management also guided to a structurally different seasonality pattern. The CFO stated that 2025 broke the historical 2020-2024 pattern of heavier Q2/Q3 revenue tied to mobile, and that 2026 sales are expected to invert relative to 2025 (with 2025 described as 40% in 1H and 60% in 2H). This statement reinforces that even as mobile becomes a smaller driver, compute and memory ordering patterns introduce a different, less predictable lumpiness, reducing the usefulness of historical intra-year seasonality heuristics.

TARGET EARNINGS MODEL AND STRATEGIC FRAME

The new target earnings model was presented as “evergreen” and tied to an ATE TAM of $12 billion to $14 billion rather than a specific year, explicitly to reflect compute and memory program timing volatility. Management’s target at that TAM assumed approximately $6 billion of revenue, gross margin 59%-61%, operating expenses 27%-29% of revenue, operating profit 30%-34%, and non-GAAP EPS $9.50 to $11. A major implication is that, relative to FY 2025 non-GAAP EPS of $3.96, the model suggests approximately 2.5x EPS potential at the modeled TAM and scale. The model also implies meaningful operating leverage (operating margin expanding from 22% in FY 2025 to 30%-34% at scale), driven by a combination of mix, scale efficiencies, and presumed R&D/SG&A leverage. (Teradyne, Inc.)

This model represents a step-up versus prior mid-term framing. In the prior-year cycle, the company referenced a mid-term model targeting 2028 revenue of $4.5 billion to $5.5 billion and non-GAAP EPS of $7 to $9.50. The new model increases the revenue scale assumption to about $6 billion and lifts the upper EPS bound to $11, while explicitly avoiding time anchoring. The lack of a fixed-year anchor reduces accountability to a calendar target but also reduces the probability that investors misinterpret the model as a linear forecast; the risk is that the market nevertheless capitalizes the higher earnings power while underweighting timing uncertainty and volatility. 

The model’s core sensitivities were explicitly identified in Q&A. The CEO described the largest uncertainty as the speed at which the market grows to the assumed TAM, citing macro/external conditions as an “X factor.” The next sensitivity identified was share in compute, where management believes it is positioned to gain share from a lower base in parts of compute, but also emphasized the competitive nature of sockets. Offsetting these uncertainties, management argued that non-core-AI drivers (mobile complexity recovery without returning to 2021 peak, auto/industrial recovery including wide-bandgap power testing enabled by an Infineon Technologies asset acquisition, IST customer diversification, and a large Robotics customer catalyst) provide a “balanced plan” that de-risks the $6 billion revenue target.

KEY Q&A DISCLOSURES THAT CHANGED THE INFORMATION SET

ATE TAM 2026 framing was explicitly quantified as a wide range. Management avoided giving a point estimate, but the CEO stated: “if you wanted to put a big wide range around it, it would be like 20% to 40% growth for the year,” explicitly tying the range width to limited 2H visibility. This remark is high-signal because it effectively provides a volatility band for 2026 industry demand and implicitly frames potential outcomes from a strong but moderating cycle (20%) to a continuation of outsized expansion (40%).

2025 SoC TAM composition was disclosed as roughly $5 billion compute, about $1 billion mobility, just under $1 billion auto/industrial, and about $700 million service; memory TAM was described as just under $1.4 billion with about $1.2 billion in DRAM and the remainder in flash. This disclosure highlights that compute is already the dominant TAM driver, increasing the importance of hyperscaler/custom ASIC and merchant compute program trajectories.

Merchant GPU opportunity timing and ramp shape were clarified. Production qualification is expected, but management emphasized complexity and timing uncertainty. Q1 guidance excludes merchant GPU revenue, with management characterizing merchant GPU contribution as more relevant to 2H 2026 and expecting initial share in the 1-digit range, potentially migrating toward a 30%/70% dual-source equilibrium over time in analogous competitive structures. The implication is that GPU is an incremental, multi-year share opportunity rather than an immediate step-function in 2026, reducing near-term upside optionality but improving credibility of guidance.

Customer concentration disclosure was updated. Management stated that in 2025 there were 2 greater-than-10% specifying customers and 1 greater-than-10% purchasing customer, each roughly around 10% rather than “substantially higher.” One specifying customer was described as mobile and 1 as compute, while the purchasing customer spans segments. This supports the view that near-term revenue is structurally exposed to a small number of decision-makers, even if purchasing channel differs from platform specification.

Robotics profitability path was reiterated with a clearer operational milestone. Management stated a target of breakeven in Robotics in 2026 and characterized the large e-commerce program as potentially ~3x 2025-to-2026 growth. The operational implication is that Robotics is still not being treated as a near-term margin contributor, but it is being positioned as a stabilizing rather than dilutive segment by the end of 2026.

INVESTMENT IMPLICATIONS

The quarter’s call materially increases the probability of a higher earnings power regime in the near term, but also increases volatility and forecasting error risk due to elevated concentration in AI-driven compute cycles.

Positive investment implications supported by call disclosures:

The magnitude of Q4 beat and Q1 guidance implies that AI-related ordering momentum accelerated late in 2025 and persisted into early 2026, with Semiconductor Test as the dominant contributor. The Q1 guide implies a new quarterly revenue record and margin expansion, suggesting that incremental volume is flowing through at attractive incremental margins even at near-record revenue levels. (Teradyne, Inc.)
AI exposure is expanding beyond core SoC and memory test into adjacent infrastructure test nodes, potentially broadening the durability of AI-linked demand and mitigating single-program dependency within Semiconductor Test. The CEO’s framing that AI is driving demand in “power management, SLT, HDD, ICT and optical test” supports the view that AI capex increasingly propagates through multiple Teradyne product lines, not just VIP compute ATE.
The medium-term earnings model, although not time-anchored, provides a higher ceiling for normalized EPS and margins than prior mid-term frameworks. If the ATE TAM reaches $12 billion to $14 billion and Teradyne reaches the modeled scale, the implied EPS power ($9.50-$11) materially exceeds the FY 2025 baseline ($3.96) and could justify a durable multiple re-rating if investors believe cycle-adjusted durability has increased. (Teradyne, Inc.)
Optionality exists in merchant GPU test share and in SLT/system-level test insertions. Management explicitly excluded GPU revenue from Q1 guidance and framed it as a 2H 2026 contributor, reducing the risk that near-term guidance embeds aggressive GPU assumptions. Over a multi-year horizon, the described ramp dynamic toward a more balanced dual-source share split implies meaningful upside if Teradyne’s next-generation instrument capability and mission-mode test advantages translate into sustained share gains.
The MultiLane JV extends strategic coverage in high-speed interconnect test, a critical vector as AI systems scale bandwidth and shift to more complex connectivity. The JV is positioned as EPS de minimis in 2026, reducing near-term execution risk while preserving strategic upside as the served market expands. (Teradyne, Inc.)

tweet.text:
Good morning!

Your reminder that with earnings, there's no free lunch. There's ALWAYS a trade-off.

Sometimes they gap for you. Sometimes against you. 

I booked $TER ahead of earnings, but it could have easily been an $STX, $BE reaction. 

I sold $VRT ahead of earnings too, and it looks fine. 

Have to understand the possibilities and accept them before they play out. 

Otherwise you're walking down a dark road of hindsight and Monday morning quarterbacking.
